body.u: Looking back over my life I haven’t really fulfilled all of my hopes and ambitions.
When I was leaving school at the age of sixteen I was quite unprepared. In school the teachers had filled my head with everything they thought I must know before leaving.

They talked about marriage, divorce and emphasised on starting a job. One lesson a week was spent talking about these things, but most of the time, we were just ignorant of the fact that it wasn’t long before we would be leaving.

We were told about how critical the job shortage was, and how we could overcome this fact. I had one appointment for an interview at the careers office for adviseadvice as did everybody. I was told about all of the jobs available and which I could apply for. The chance of me getting the job which I hoped for was almost nil, as there were many other school leavers applying to the same firm.

I had filled in a questionnaire about myself to help the interviewer. I was interviewed by a woman and she was very helpful. It was quite a well organised careers centre, plus, which I think was a very good idea the centre was on the school grounds. I think that all schools should have a careers office or centre on their grounds.

I was given plenty of forms to send of for jobs and more interviews. Really I think that schools should make mock interviews for separate students to get them used to it. I was given only one interview which lasted only twenty-five minutes. The interviewers should also keep in touch with some of the students to see how they get on. I did obtain an apprenticeship in what I wanted but there still seems to have been something missing. Most of the things which I did were different to what the school advised. I retired on a pension at sixty-five.
